passages.text: It chanced once on a time my mind was meditating on the things that are, my thought was raised to a great height, the senses of my body being held back — just as men who are weighed down with sleep after a full meal, or from fatigue of body.
Methought a Being more than vast, in size beyond all bounds, called out my name and saith: What wouldst thou hear and see, and what hast thou in mind to learn and know?
And I do say: Who art thou? He saith: I am Poemandres, Mind of the Sovereignty; I know what thou desirest and I am with thee everywhere.

I reply: I long to learn the things that are, and comprehend their nature, and know God. This is my desire; and I would hear. He answered back to me: Hold in thy mind all thou wouldst know, and I will teach thee.
Now Light and Life are God and Father, whence Man was born. If then thou learnest that thou art thyself of Life and Light, and that thou happenest to be out of them, thou shalt return again to Life.
I am that Light, the Mind, thy God, who am before the moist nature that appeared out of darkness; and the bright shining Word from Mind is Son of God.
Thus understand: That which in thee sees and hears the Word of the Lord, and that which understands the Father — they are not different from one another; they are united in the Light.
The greatest ill among mankind is ignorance of God. And next to this, the second greatest is that men do not know themselves.
For those who know, they are beloved of God, and God gives them the gift of gnosis — that knowledge of the Divine nature which transforms the knower.
The soul of man is deathless. It passes through the seven spheres, shedding its planetary influences, until it merges with the Ogdoad — the realm beyond the planets, where the blessed souls abide.

Hymn to the Almighty: Holy is God the Father of all things. Holy is God, whose will is performed and accomplished by his own Powers. Holy is God, who determineth to be known, and is known of his own, and to his own.
Mind, the Father of all, being Life and Light, brought forth Man like to himself, of whom he was enamored as being his own child; for Man was very goodly, bearing the image of his Father.

In very truth, God was enamored of his own form; and on him did He bestow all of His own workmanships. And when Man had observed what the Creator had constructed in the Fire, he himself wished also to make some work, and permission was given him by the Father.

So Man, having seen the creation of the Craftsman, wished to make things too. And the Father consented. Man descended into the fabricated sphere, having all authority over the mortal world and over the irrational creatures.
